cases.title: Opening My First Staffing Agency Without Industry Experience
cases.subtitle: Starting from zero in a new industry, with no clients, no candidates and no established local operation.
cases.caption: Contres, France — industrial and food production environment
cases.takeaway: A new industry can be learned fast when the field becomes the classroom.
cases.body:
Context

My first major experience in the staffing industry began when I was recruited as a Branch Manager to open a new SAMSIC staffing agency in Contres, a small town in the Loir-et-Cher region of France.

I did not come from the staffing industry. I had commercial experience, training experience and a strong understanding of field-based professions, but I had never managed a staffing agency. I had been a temporary worker myself in the past, which gave me awareness of the candidate side, but not the full mechanics of running a staffing branch.

The challenge

The agency started with no active clients, no candidate database and no established local operation. The obvious accounts were already working with other staffing agencies, and several initially refused to add another provider.

What I did

I mapped the local market and started with sectors where I could speak credibly. My earlier experience in welding, metal fabrication and manufacturing became a real advantage. When I interviewed a welder, I could ask about TIG, MIG, plan reading, folding machines and production skills. That gave credibility with both candidates and employers.

I used a candidate-led selling approach. Instead of waiting for job orders, I looked for strong candidates first, then created the opportunity by presenting them directly to companies.

When you have a strong candidate, you do not wait for the perfect job order. You actively create the opportunity.

I also prospected early in the morning, when industrial decision-makers were present and less surrounded by the usual sales noise.

Outcome

The agency gained traction by placing candidates one by one and building trust with employers. That experience became the foundation of my later management philosophy: start from the field, understand the real work, and turn small wins into a repeatable commercial system.

cases.title: Scaling a Region from Zero to 13 Agencies
cases.subtitle: Building a regional staffing network across Southern France where the company had no established presence.
cases.caption: SAMSIC Nice — one of the first openings in the PACA expansion
cases.takeaway: Expansion is not one strategy. It is a sequence of local executions.
cases.body:
Context

After successfully launching my first agency, I was promoted to Regional Director at SAMSIC with responsibility for developing the PACA region in Southern France. The territory was essentially empty for the staffing division. The initial objective was to open four to five agencies, with more to follow if the model worked.

The challenge

The first agency was opened in Nice because another SAMSIC business unit already had a local security operation. We expected this internal connection to help us access a strategic airport client. It did not work that way. Even within the same group, the local operation had its own obligations and could not simply open the door for us. It took nearly eighteen months before we placed our first temporary worker there.

Every market we entered was already competitive. Local staffing firms had existing relationships, candidate pools and client trust. We had to build credibility from scratch.

What I did

I treated each opening as a local execution problem. Nice, Cannes, Toulon, Marseille, Fos-sur-Mer and the surrounding areas did not have the same labor market, the same sectors or the same relationship dynamics.

I recruited people who could understand their territory quickly, speak to local companies and build trust. Local knowledge, communication skills and commercial energy were often more important than a perfect staffing resume.

Expansion is not only a strategy. It is a sequence of local executions.

Outcome

The region expanded to 13 agencies across Southern France, covering industrial, logistics, maritime, hospitality and service markets. The business grew from zero regional presence to a structured network with significant revenue and team scale.

cases.title: Building a Repeatable Agency Launch System
cases.subtitle: Turning agency openings into a structured operating model instead of relying on improvisation.
cases.caption: Training and onboarding system — from improvisation to repeatable launch process
cases.body:
Context

Opening a staffing agency from scratch means immediate financial pressure: no clients, no candidates, no local reputation and fixed costs from day one. Official group training existed, but new hires were not always able to attend immediately. We needed people productive now, not in several weeks.

The challenge

If every opening depends on improvisation, growth becomes fragile. I needed a model that could accelerate learning and make performance more consistent across branches.

What I did

I used my training background to create an internal pre-training process. It included a small training guide, daily topics, practical exercises, short knowledge checks and immediate field application.

The program covered resume reading, candidate evaluation, commercial routines, competitive watch, job posting analysis, employment law basics and how to turn a good candidate into a placement opportunity.

I also used group recruitment sessions with practical exercises: presenting another candidate, writing a job ad, analyzing employment law situations and solving realistic agency scenarios.

Outcome

The method became a repeatable launch model. It allowed me to recruit outside the industry and still make new employees operational quickly. It also created consistency across openings and supported faster progress toward the first profitability thresholds.

cases.title: Why I Stopped Hiring Only for Experience
cases.subtitle: How practical exercises helped me identify potential beyond industry keywords.
cases.caption: Recruitment — evaluating potential, not only previous job titles
cases.takeaway: The perfect resume is not always the best predictor. The work itself must be tested.
cases.body:
Context

As I opened and developed staffing agencies, I quickly saw the limits of recruiting only through previous experience. Experience was useful, but it did not always predict performance. Some candidates had the right industry keywords on their resume but lacked energy, curiosity, communication or ownership. Others had no direct background, but showed the attitude, reasoning and learning speed needed to become strong performers.

In a staffing agency, performance depends on more than knowing the vocabulary of the business. People need to listen, understand jobs quickly, communicate clearly, evaluate candidates, handle clients, write accurate job ads, manage priorities and stay active under pressure.

The challenge

If I hired only people who had already worked in staffing, I reduced one risk but created another: I could miss stronger profiles simply because they did not come from the exact industry.

The real question became: how do you identify people who can learn the job fast, behave well with clients and candidates, and become productive with the right training?

The perfect resume is not always the best predictor. The work itself must be tested.

What I changed

I started using collective interviews and practical exercises instead of relying only on traditional one-on-one interviews. The goal was not to make the process harder. The goal was to make potential visible.

I first explained the reality of the job: the pace, the pressure, the need to switch between candidates, clients, job orders, legal details and commercial action. Then I observed how people reacted when the work became concrete.

I used practical situations such as:

  • presenting another candidate to evaluate listening, synthesis and communication,
  • reading a resume and identifying what mattered,
  • writing a job advertisement from a simple client need,
  • analyzing a basic employment-law situation,
  • explaining how they would approach a client or a candidate,
  • solving realistic agency scenarios under time pressure.

What I was looking for

I was not looking for perfect answers. I was looking for signals: curiosity, structure, common sense, listening quality, clarity of speech, commercial instinct, attention to detail, humility and the ability to learn from feedback.

This approach helped separate people who only looked good on paper from people who could actually grow into the work.

Outcome

The method allowed me to recruit stronger non-traditional profiles and make them operational through training, routines and field support. It also gave me a more reliable way to evaluate future managers, assistants and commercial profiles.

Instead of asking only, "Has this person already done the job?" I learned to ask better questions:

  • Can this person understand the work?
  • Can this person communicate clearly?
  • Can this person learn fast?
  • Can this person handle pressure?
  • Can this person become strong with the right system?

Why this case matters

This case matters because it shows how I reduce the risk of hiring for potential. I do not simply say, "Let us give someone a chance." I build a process that reveals whether the chance is justified.

That philosophy has followed me since: define the real work, test the behaviors that matter, train what can be taught, and select for the qualities that are harder to create later.

cases.title: Turning an Assistant into a Branch Manager
cases.subtitle: Recognizing performance before the title exists.
cases.caption: SAMSIC Cannes — the early team and the future branch manager
cases.takeaway: The role can follow the result.
cases.body:
Context

In one new agency, the person creating the most value did not hold the most senior title. The assistant was not officially the commercial leader, yet she was the one showing energy, relationship skills and real placement results.

The challenge

I had to decide whether to replace the manager with another external profile or build around the person already demonstrating potential.

What I did

I created a measurable opportunity. I gave her a clear target and a clear path: if she could generate ten additional placements, she would move into a commercial role and we would recruit support around her.

Show the result, and the role will follow.

Outcome

She reached the target in one month. She was promoted, continued to develop and later became Branch Manager. This reinforced a management principle I have kept since: when someone is already doing the work, the title should not be the barrier.

cases.title: Building Accountability Without Micromanagement
cases.subtitle: Making performance visible across a multi-site organization.
cases.caption: Performance indicators — making activity visible without turning management into surveillance
cases.takeaway: If you cannot measure the activity, you cannot understand the weakness.
cases.body:
Context

When I joined ISA as Regional Director, I inherited agencies that were already operating. The previous leader was moving up into a higher role, and I was taking over teams that had not chosen me. That made the transition sensitive.

The challenge

Some agencies were active and developing. Others appeared stable on paper, but much of the performance came from inherited client portfolios rather than new commercial activity. Without indicators, it was difficult to know whether the issue was skill, process, support or willingness.

What I did

I introduced simple performance indicators: number of visits, candidate submissions, new clients, quality of candidate pools and daily commercial activity. I explained that the point was not to blame people. The point was to understand where support was needed.

If there was a weakness, my role was to help fix it. But if nothing was being done, the indicators made that visible too.

Outcome

For motivated teams, the system created structure and support. For others, it clarified that the role required active development, not only maintenance of an existing portfolio. It helped build a more honest performance culture across the region.

cases.title: Leading Through Resistance to Change
cases.subtitle: Taking over established teams without triggering unnecessary conflict.
cases.caption: Change leadership — helping people accept a more visible performance culture
cases.takeaway: Change is accepted faster when people understand how it helps them win.
cases.body:
Context

At ISA, I stepped into a role where people already had habits, loyalties and expectations. Some team members were naturally cautious. Others were resistant because new visibility could expose weak commercial activity.

The challenge

If I pushed too hard, people could reject the change or escalate complaints upward. If I avoided the issue, performance would not improve.

What I did

I framed performance indicators as a tool for support. More activity, better candidate pools and stronger commercial development helped the agency, the region and the people inside the agency. When necessary, I also had direct conversations about the difference between managing a portfolio and actively developing a business.

Outcome

Some people engaged with the new logic and improved. Others realized that the environment no longer matched what they wanted. That created clearer alignment and reduced the gap between apparent performance and real contribution.

cases.title: Winning Accounts Everyone Else Had Given Up On
cases.subtitle: How understanding the real obstacle helped open an account that initially refused to work with us.
cases.caption: Center Parcs Sologne — a staffing problem hidden inside a transportation problem
cases.takeaway: The client did not only have a recruitment problem. They had a logistics problem.
cases.body:
Context

Center Parcs was one of the major local targets near my first agency. They needed staff, but they already worked with staffing providers and initially refused to add another agency.

The challenge

The real issue was not only recruitment. It was logistics and motivation. The site was about 30 to 45 minutes away, the assignments were often part-time, physically demanding and paid at a low hourly rate. For many candidates, the trip did not make economic sense.

What I did

When the client eventually called because other providers could not serve an urgent need, I understood that this was the entry point. I provided a candidate quickly, but I also changed the proposition.

Instead of sending one or two isolated workers, I proposed groups. The workers organized carpooling themselves, and the client committed to taking a minimum number of people at once. That made the assignment more viable for candidates and more reliable for the client.

The client did not only have a recruitment problem. They had a logistics problem.

Outcome

The account opened, and the agency was able to take more and more space as a provider. The lesson was simple: sometimes the way to win a client is not to promise harder recruitment, but to remove the practical constraint that prevents people from accepting the work.

cases.title: Why I Worked as a Waiter and Kitchen Assistant to Solve a Staffing Problem
cases.subtitle: Testing the job myself to understand the real hiring criteria.
cases.caption: ZooParc de Beauval hotels — redefining the profile instead of searching harder
cases.takeaway: When the labor pool is exhausted, redefine the profile instead of searching harder for the same person.
cases.body:
Context

ZooParc de Beauval was another major local labor consumer. The park and its hotels needed servers, kitchen assistants, cooks and hospitality staff. But when candidates heard the name, many immediately refused because they had already worked there or had a negative perception of the assignments.

The challenge

The standard approach was to look for experienced hospitality profiles. But the local pool was limited, and the same profiles had already been overused by the market.

What I did

I asked the client if I could take the assignments myself. I worked as a server and as a kitchen assistant, not only to observe but to actually help during real needs.

I wanted to understand exactly what the work required. Serving plates, clearing tables, staying alert, preparing simple kitchen tasks, cutting vegetables, keeping things clean and following instructions were not always tasks that required years of hospitality school. Many required reliability, presentation, attention and willingness to learn.

When there is a labor shortage, the solution is not always to search harder for the same profile. Sometimes the solution is to redefine the profile.

Outcome

I worked with the client to broaden the acceptable profile. I selected people carefully for attitude, seriousness and presentation, then asked the client to onboard them step by step. This helped place more candidates and develop the account.

cases.title: Challenging Assumptions in Recruitment
cases.subtitle: Looking at what the job really requires instead of what the job description claims to require.
cases.caption: Recruitment process — practical assessment instead of perfect-resume hunting
cases.takeaway: The perfect candidate often does not exist. The right candidate often needs a better definition.
cases.body:
Context

Across staffing, I repeatedly saw organizations searching for the perfect candidate: exact title, exact industry, exact experience and exact background. But when I looked at the real daily work, many tasks were narrower and easier to teach than the job description suggested.

The challenge

By over-defining the ideal profile, companies rejected people who could perform well with proper onboarding. This was especially damaging in tight labor markets.

What I did

I broke roles down into concrete tasks. What must the person know today? What can be learned quickly? What behaviors are non-negotiable?

I used practical assessments: presenting another candidate, writing a job ad, reading a resume, analyzing simple employment law cases and solving real agency situations. This helped reveal communication, attention, reasoning and commercial instinct.

Outcome

The approach reduced dependency on perfect resumes and opened access to stronger potential. It also became part of my broader management philosophy: define the work clearly before defining the candidate.

cases.title: Designing a Staffing Platform Before Becoming a Developer
cases.subtitle: Using a no-code prototype to make an operational problem visible and understandable.
cases.caption: SAMSIC digital environment — transforming field frustration into product thinking
cases.takeaway: A good prototype makes the problem impossible to ignore.
proof: Business process translated into product logic
cases.body:
Context

While managing a staffing agency, I noticed that the handling of candidate applications was inefficient. We had resumes, candidate information and business needs, but the workflow around receiving, classifying, sending and tracking applications could be much better.

The challenge

I was not a developer at the time. I was a field manager seeing an operational problem. I needed a way to make the solution visible enough that decision-makers could understand it.

What I did

I used Apple Keynote to build an interactive prototype with linked slides, screens, buttons and simulated application behavior. It looked and behaved like a digital application: candidate library, application processing, client submission and workflow triggers.

A good prototype does not need to be technically perfect. It needs to make the future process understandable.

Outcome

The prototype turned an abstract operational idea into something concrete. It allowed leadership to see what the tool could do, how it would work and why the field needed it.

cases.title: Convincing a CEO to Support a New Digital Solution
cases.subtitle: Turning a field-level idea into a company-level digital project.
cases.caption: From branch-level idea to group-level digital project
cases.takeaway: Field ideas can become company tools when they are made clear enough.
cases.body:
Context

After building the prototype, I faced a choice. I could keep it at branch level or take the risk of presenting it to senior leadership.

The challenge

I was not part of IT. I was not formally responsible for digital tools. Presenting such a project could have been seen as stepping outside my role.

What I did

At a regional meeting, I presented the prototype directly to the CEO, Thierry Geoffroy. I was nervous, but the prototype made the idea tangible. The response was very positive. He recognized the depth of the work and connected me with IT leadership to continue the project.

Outcome

The concept was retained and developed into an internal tool. I became involved as a business-side reference point, helping connect field realities with system design.

cases.title: From 280 to 650 Members: The Growth Lesson That Shaped My Management Style
cases.subtitle: Relaunching a fitness club through visible change, Les Mills programs, sales discipline and the cash-flow lesson that shaped how I manage growth.
cases.caption: Les Mills programs - turning group fitness into a community and acquisition engine
cases.takeaway: Growth must be designed, not just created.
cases.body:
The situation

I was 25 years old when I took over a fitness club in Blois, France.

The business was not broken. It had around 280 active members, mostly on traditional annual memberships. The club had a real base, regular clients and a stable local presence.

But it also felt static.

The activity was balanced, but not dynamic. The equipment was aging. The reception area lacked energy. The locker rooms needed work. The group exercise room had potential, but it was not being used as a real commercial engine.

I did not want to simply maintain the business. I wanted to relaunch it.

My first instinct was clear: if I wanted people to believe that something had changed, they had to see and feel that something had changed.

The challenge

The challenge was not only to attract more members. It was to change the energy of the place.

I had limited financial resources, so I could not transform everything at once. Every small profit had to be reinvested carefully.

The real question was:

How do you create momentum in a business that is functioning, but not growing?

I decided to work on three levers at the same time:

  • The physical environment
  • The member experience
  • The commercial offer

What I changed first

I started with visible improvements.

We repainted the reception area. We improved the locker rooms. We made the space feel clearer, more modern and more welcoming. We reorganized and upgraded what we could with limited means.

The goal was not luxury. The goal was signal.

I wanted existing members to feel that the club was moving forward, and I wanted new prospects to immediately understand that this was not the same old gym anymore.

The major operational shift: Les Mills

The biggest transformation came through group fitness.

I invested in Les Mills programs and introduced several concepts into the club: RPM, BodyCombat, BodyAttack, BodyPump, BodyJam and BodyStep.

This was a major decision.

It required investment, training and adaptation from the instructors. It also changed the identity of the club. Some members initially complained, because the courses became more structured, more intense and more branded.

But I believed in the direction.

Les Mills brought energy, consistency, progression, music, marketing power and a stronger sense of community. It also solved a major operational issue for instructors: instead of constantly inventing new classes, they could rely on a professional system, updated every three months.

I distributed the programs according to each instructor's strengths and preferences. I tried to make the transition realistic by giving each instructor two or three concepts maximum to learn and master.

This was not just a product change. It was a team transformation.

The commercial turning point

At the same time, I trained myself.

I joined a Les Mills sales training program called Supersonic, led at the time by Jean-Philippe Perez. That training changed the way I saw sales.

It helped me understand that selling was not about forcing someone to buy. It was about removing friction, creating urgency, making the offer easy to understand, and helping people take action.

That was the moment I discovered something important about myself: I was not only comfortable selling - I was good at it.

So I changed the way we presented memberships.

My objective was simple: fill the club.

I believed that people attract people. A full gym creates energy. A full class creates desire. A full room creates proof.

So I created a duo offer.

The idea was simple:

I will make it easier for you to join, but you need to come with someone.

This worked because fitness is often social. Many people talk about starting but do not act. I wanted to give them the perfect argument:

Come with me. If we join together, we get a better price.

The offer reduced hesitation and created immediate referrals.

The result

The impact was massive.

The club grew from around 280 members to approximately 650 members within a few months.

Group classes changed completely. Sessions that used to gather around 18 people started attracting more than 40.

The club became alive.

The atmosphere changed. The rooms were full. The courses had energy. Members talked about the club. The team felt the momentum.

For a period, the gym became one of the most dynamic fitness clubs in the area.

The mistake

But growth creates pressure. And I made one major financial mistake.

Because I wanted zero friction at the point of sale, I accepted payment plans too easily: four payments, eight payments, twelve payments - whatever helped the person sign.

Commercially, it worked. Financially, it created a cash-flow gap that became extremely difficult to absorb.

I had generated growth, but I had not protected the financial structure behind that growth.

That lesson stayed with me.

Revenue is not cash.
Growth is not security.
Momentum is not management.

A business can grow fast and still become fragile if the operating model does not support the commercial strategy.

Eventually, I had to exit the business and sell the gym to a previous employer, someone I knew from my earlier years as a swimming instructor and aquagym coach.

The second lesson: transmission

What happened next was just as important as the growth itself.

After the sale, the new owner also adopted Les Mills programs. I helped with the transition and trained his team in the commercial methods I had learned and applied.

That moment revealed another part of my professional identity.

I enjoyed selling.
I enjoyed building.
But I loved transmitting.

I loved taking something I had learned, turning it into a practical method, and helping others use it.

That experience became one of the foundations of my management style.

What this case taught me

This case shaped me deeply.

It taught me that energy matters, but structure matters more.

It taught me that a strong commercial idea can change a business quickly.

It taught me that training a team is not a cost; it is a growth engine.

It taught me that customer acquisition must always be connected to cash discipline.

And it taught me that my strongest contribution is often not only doing things myself, but turning what I learn into something others can repeat.

What I would do differently today

I would still make the transformation.

I would still modernize the club.

I would still invest in the team.

I would still use group fitness as a community and acquisition engine.

I would still create a referral-based offer.

But I would structure the payment model differently.

I would protect cash flow.

I would separate commercial flexibility from financial exposure.

I would build dashboards earlier: signed revenue, collected cash, payment risk, retention, class attendance, instructor load, acquisition source and member lifetime value.

In other words, I would keep the ambition - but I would build the financial control system around it from day one.

Why this case matters

This story is important because it shows both sides of growth.

It shows my ability to enter an existing environment, identify what lacks energy, change the experience, train people, create a commercial offer and generate rapid traction.

But it also shows the lesson that made me more disciplined later.

I learned that growth must be designed, not just created.

That lesson has followed me in every operational project since then.

Build momentum.
Create systems.
Train people.
Protect cash.
Make growth repeatable.

cases.title: Building a Training Organization from Scratch
cases.subtitle: Creating a professional training business with compliance, curriculum design and commercial development.
cases.caption: Professional training organization — curriculum, certification and business development
cases.takeaway: Training is strongest when people leave with tools they can use immediately.
cases.body:
Context

I founded Ofise Formation, a professional training organization focused on leadership, sales, management and business development.

The challenge

Building a training organization required more than content creation. It required quality certification, administrative compliance, trainer coordination, client acquisition and curriculum design.

What I did

I designed training programs, recruited expert trainers and built a structure that met national quality expectations. My focus was always practical: people had to leave with tools they could use immediately.

Outcome

The organization obtained the required quality certification framework and generated more than €100K in revenue within 18 months.

cases.title: Building a Service Business in the United States
cases.subtitle: Starting over in a new country and building a local field-services company from zero.
cases.caption: Starting over — rebuilding operating principles in a new market
cases.takeaway: Starting over tests whether the operating principles still work in a new market.
cases.body:
Context

After relocating to Florida, I founded Florida Eco Services, a residential and commercial field-services company.

The challenge

Building a business in a new country meant no local brand, no inherited customer base, different market expectations, different acquisition channels and a new operating environment.

What I did

I managed the full operation: customer acquisition, estimates, pricing, scheduling, quality control, service delivery and reputation management. I also developed digital workflows to improve consistency and customer communication.

Outcome

The company grew to more than $120K annualized revenue within two years while building a strong local reputation.

cases.title: Improving Logistics Traceability in an Aerospace and Defense Environment
cases.subtitle: Designing and building a scan-based tracking application to replace fragmented parcel visibility.
cases.caption: Receiving Incident Control Tower — internal scan workflow interface
cases.takeaway: If a parcel can move, the system must know who touched it, when, and where it went.
cases.body:
Context

In an aerospace and defense logistics environment, parts and parcels were received every day. The team scanned codes into Excel, which helped count volume but did not provide reliable traceability after reception.

When a parcel had to be located, people could spend significant time searching because the process did not clearly show where the item had gone, who processed it or whether it had been returned.

The challenge

The issue was not the absence of effort. The issue was the absence of a traceability system. Excel could record entries, but it could not easily answer the operational question: where is this item now?

What I built

I designed and coded a scan-based internal tracking application. The system uses QR codes to identify zones, scan parcel or part references, timestamp entries, capture the user, track where the item is routed and manage returns.

A user can search for a parcel number and immediately see when it entered, who received it and where it was sent. The tool also provides statistics by person, day, week, month or selected date range.

Authentication was secured through Cloudflare, making the tool reliable and controlled for internal use.

Outcome

The application is now used internally. It replaced a fragmented visibility process with a practical traceability workflow and made searching for parcels significantly faster and more reliable.
